owaspTop10.title: Broken Access Control
owaspTop10.what: People can access information or actions they shouldn’t, like viewing someone else’s account or editing data without permission.
owaspTop10.why: Attackers could steal personal information, change important data, or take over user accounts.
owaspTop10.how: Ensure users can only access what they’re allowed to by enforcing permissions on the server side.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like someone walking into a locked office because the door was accidentally left open.

owaspTop10.title: Weak Data Protection
owaspTop10.what: Sensitive data, like passwords or credit card numbers, isn’t properly secured.
owaspTop10.why: If attackers gain access, they can easily read and misuse this information.
owaspTop10.how: Encrypt sensitive data, use HTTPS, and never store passwords in plain text.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like sending a postcard with your bank details written on it for anyone to read.

owaspTop10.title: Injection Attacks
owaspTop10.what: Attackers send harmful input (like code) into a website that gets executed by the system.
owaspTop10.why: This can allow attackers to access, modify, or delete data, or even take control of the system.
owaspTop10.how: Validate user input and use secure coding practices like prepared statements for databases.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like tricking a cashier into entering a secret command instead of ringing up an item.

owaspTop10.title: Insecure Design
owaspTop10.what: The application is built without considering security from the beginning.
owaspTop10.why: Even if it works, it may contain built-in weaknesses that attackers can exploit.
owaspTop10.how: Plan for security early by thinking through potential risks during the design phase.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like building a house without locks and trying to add them later.

owaspTop10.title: Security Misconfiguration
owaspTop10.what: System settings are left in an unsafe state, such as using default passwords or leaving features exposed.
owaspTop10.why: Attackers can take advantage of these easy-to-find weaknesses to gain access.
owaspTop10.how: Secure configurations by changing defaults, disabling unused features, and regularly reviewing settings.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like leaving your front door unlocked with a sign that says ‘key under the mat.’

owaspTop10.title: Outdated or Vulnerable Software
owaspTop10.what: The application uses old or unpatched software, libraries, or plugins.
owaspTop10.why: Attackers can exploit known vulnerabilities that already have fixes available.
owaspTop10.how: Keep all software up to date and remove anything that is no longer needed.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like using an old lock that burglars already know how to pick.

owaspTop10.title: Authentication Failures
owaspTop10.what: Problems with login systems, such as weak passwords or poor session management.
owaspTop10.why: Attackers can easily gain access to user accounts and impersonate others.
owaspTop10.how: Use strong passwords, enable multi-factor authentication, and properly manage user sessions.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like using ‘1234’ as a lock combination on your front door.

owaspTop10.title: Data Integrity Failures
owaspTop10.what: The system trusts data or software that could be tampered with.
owaspTop10.why: Attackers could insert malicious updates or alter important data without being detected.
owaspTop10.how: Verify the integrity of data and software using trusted sources and validation methods.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like installing a package that’s been opened and altered without checking it first.

owaspTop10.title: Logging and Monitoring Failures
owaspTop10.what: The system does not properly track or alert on suspicious activity.
owaspTop10.why: Attacks can go unnoticed, allowing more damage before anyone responds.
owaspTop10.how: Log important actions and monitor systems to quickly detect and respond to threats.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like having no security cameras or alarms in a store.

owaspTop10.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
owaspTop10.what: Attackers trick a server into making requests to unintended locations.
owaspTop10.why: This can expose internal systems or sensitive data that should not be publicly accessible.
owaspTop10.how: Restrict and validate outgoing requests to ensure only safe destinations are allowed.
owaspTop10.analogy: Like convincing a receptionist to fetch confidential files from a restricted room.
